expanded_description: Applications of right triangle trigonometry bring trigonometry to life by showing how it solves real-world problems. From measuring heights of buildings you can't climb, to finding distances across rivers you can't cross, to navigating ships and aircraft, right triangle trigonometry is indispensable. This section covers the major applications: angles of elevation (looking up at something), angles of depression (looking down from something), bearings (navigation directions), and indirect measurement (finding measurements you can't measure directly). Each application uses the same core tools—SOH CAH TOA—but applies them to different scenarios. Understanding these applications shows why trigonometry matters—it's not just abstract math but a practical tool used daily in surveying, navigation, engineering, construction, and physics. In Pre-calculus, mastering applications builds problem-solving skills, develops ability to translate word problems into mathematical models, and prepares students for calculus applications (related rates, optimization). The problem-solving strategy (draw diagram, label known/unknown, choose trig ratio, solve, check) works for all trig applications. This section teaches applications—angles of elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement, and systematic problem-solving approach. Understanding applications builds appreciation for trigonometry as practical tool.

core_concept.principle:
Applications of Right Triangle Trig:

1. Angles of Elevation:
Angle measured upward from horizontal
Used to find heights of buildings, trees, mountains
Angle between horizontal line of sight and object above

2. Angles of Depression:
Angle measured downward from horizontal
Used to find distances from elevated positions
Angle between horizontal line of sight and object below
Equal to angle of elevation from object (alternate interior angles)

3. Bearings:
Direction measured from north or south
N 30° E: 30° east of north
S 45° W: 45° west of south
Used in navigation, surveying

4. Indirect Measurement:
Find heights/distances you can't measure directly
Use trig ratios with known angle and distance
Shadow problems, height problems, distance problems

Problem-Solving Strategy:
1. Draw accurate diagram
2. Label known information
3. Identify right triangle
4. Choose appropriate trig ratio (SOH CAH TOA)
5. Solve for unknown
6. Check if answer is reasonable
core_concept.explanation: Think of trig applications as real-world puzzles—you're given some information (angles, distances) and need to find something else (height, distance). The key is drawing a good diagram—this makes the problem clear. Then identify the right triangle—most applications involve right triangles. Then choose the right trig ratio—SOH CAH TOA tells you which. Then solve—algebra to find the unknown. Finally, check—does the answer make sense? This strategy works for all trig applications. Angles of elevation/depression are complementary—if you're looking up at 30°, someone at the top looking down sees 30° too (alternate interior angles). Bearings use a different angle system—measured from north or south, not from positive x-axis. Indirect measurement is powerful—you can find heights of buildings, widths of rivers, distances to ships without directly measuring. Understanding applications shows why trigonometry matters—it solves real problems. This section teaches applications—elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement, and problem-solving strategy.

image.alt: Overview of right triangle trigonometry applications
image.caption: Figure 7.8.1: Visual summary of right triangle trigonometry applications: angles of elevation/depression, bearings, and indirect measurement with example diagrams.

sections.introduction: Angles of elevation and depression are angles measured from the horizontal. Angle of elevation is measured upward (looking up at something), angle of depression is measured downward (looking down from something). These angles are used constantly in surveying, construction, navigation, and physics. Understanding the relationship between them (they're equal when measured between same two points) is key.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to angles of elevation and depression
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.8.2: Introducing angles of elevation (looking up) and depression (looking down) measured from horizontal.

key_concept.definition:
Angle of Elevation:
Angle between horizontal line and line of sight to object above
Measured upward from horizontal
Always acute angle (0° to 90°)

Angle of Depression:
Angle between horizontal line and line of sight to object below
Measured downward from horizontal
Always acute angle (0° to 90°)

Key Relationship:
Angle of elevation from point A to point B equals
Angle of depression from point B to point A
(Alternate interior angles with parallel horizontal lines)

Common Applications:
- Finding height of building, tree, mountain
- Finding distance to object
- Surveying and construction
- Navigation and aviation
- Physics (projectile motion)

Problem Setup:
1. Draw horizontal reference line
2. Draw line of sight at given angle
3. Form right triangle
4. Label known/unknown
5. Use trig ratios to solve
key_concept.context: Elevation and depression angles are practical—surveyors use them to measure land, builders use them to design structures, pilots use them for navigation. The equality of elevation and depression angles (alternate interior angles) is a key insight—it means you can measure from either end. In applications, you often know the angle and one distance, need to find another distance. The right triangle model works because we measure from horizontal. Understanding these angles builds practical trig skills—you can solve real measurement problems. This section teaches elevation/depression angles—definitions, relationship, and problem-solving.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.3: Diagram showing angle of elevation and angle of depression with horizontal reference lines and alternate interior angle relationship.

worked_examples.title: Finding Height Using Angle of Elevation

image.alt: Example finding height using angle of elevation
image.caption: Figure 7.8.4: Using angle of elevation to find height of building.

worked_examples.problem: From a point 50 feet from a building, the angle of elevation to the top is 60°. Find the height of the building.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with angle of elevation.
step_by_step.calculation:
Horizontal distance: 50 ft (adjacent)
Angle of elevation: 60°
Height: h (opposite, unknown)

step_by_step.explanation: Have adjacent, need opposite. Use tangent.

step_by_step.calculation: 86.6 ft is reasonable for building height

worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle of elevation: draw right triangle, use tan = opp/adj to find height.

worked_examples.title: Finding Distance Using Angle of Depression

image.alt: Example finding distance using angle of depression
image.caption: Figure 7.8.5: Using angle of depression to find distance from cliff.

worked_examples.problem: From a cliff 100 meters high, the angle of depression to a boat is 25°. Find the distance from the base of the cliff to the boat.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with angle of depression.
step_by_step.calculation:
Cliff height: 100 m (opposite)
Angle of depression: 25°
Distance: d (adjacent, unknown)

step_by_step.explanation: Angle of depression = angle of elevation (alternate interior angles).
Have opposite, need adjacent. Use tangent.
Multiply by d, divide by tan 25°.

step_by_step.calculation: 214.5 m is reasonable distance from cliff

worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle of depression: draw right triangle, angle of depression = angle of elevation, use tan to find distance.

worked_examples.title: Two-Angle Elevation Problem

image.caption: Figure 7.8.6: Using two angles of elevation to find height.

worked_examples.problem: From point A, the angle of elevation to a tower is 30°. From point B, 100 feet closer to the tower, the angle of elevation is 45°. Find the height of the tower.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw two right triangles sharing the height.
step_by_step.calculation:
Tower height: h
Distance from B to tower: x
Distance from A to tower: x + 100

From point B: tan 45° = h/x
From point A: tan 30° = h/(x + 100)

step_by_step.explanation: Replace h with x in second equation.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Two-angle problems: set up two equations with two unknowns, solve system to find height.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.7: Summary of angles of elevation and depression applications.

sections.introduction: Bearings are directions used in navigation and surveying. Unlike standard position angles (measured counterclockwise from positive x-axis), bearings are measured from north or south toward east or west. Understanding bearings is essential for navigation problems, surveying applications, and any problem involving directions.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.8.8: Introducing bearing notation and how bearings are measured from north or south.

key_concept.definition:
Bearing Notation:
Measured from north or south toward east or west

N θ E: θ degrees east of north
N θ W: θ degrees west of north
S θ E: θ degrees east of south
S θ W: θ degrees west of south

Examples:
N 30° E: 30° east of north (bearing = 30°)
S 45° W: 45° west of south (bearing = 225°)
N 60° W: 60° west of north (bearing = 300°)

Converting to Standard Position:
From north clockwise (navigation bearing):
N θ E = θ°
S θ E = 180° - θ°
S θ W = 180° + θ°
N θ W = 360° - θ°

Common Applications:
- Ship and aircraft navigation
- Surveying and land measurement
- Search and rescue operations
- Military operations
- Hiking and orienteering
key_concept.context: Bearings are practical—ships and planes use them for navigation, surveyors use them for land measurement, search and rescue uses them for locating people. The notation (N 30° E) is different from standard position angles—measured from north or south, not from positive x-axis. Understanding bearings builds practical navigation skills. In applications, you often need to convert between bearing notation and standard position for calculations. The right triangle model still applies—bearings create right triangles with north-south and east-west components. Understanding bearings builds practical trig skills—you can solve real navigation problems. This section teaches bearings—notation, conversion, and problem-solving.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.9: Diagram showing bearing notation (N θ E, S θ W, etc.) and how to convert to standard position angles.

worked_examples.title: Navigation Problem with Bearing

image.caption: Figure 7.8.10: Using bearing to find position in navigation.

worked_examples.problem: A ship sails 100 km on a bearing of N 60° E. How far north and how far east does it travel?

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with bearing angle.
step_by_step.calculation:
Hypotenuse: 100 km
Angle from north: 60°
North distance: n (adjacent)
East distance: e (opposite)

step_by_step.explanation: Use cos = adj/hyp for north component.
Use sin = opp/hyp for east component.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Bearings: draw right triangle, use sin/cos to find components (north/south, east/west).

worked_examples.title: Finding Bearing from Position

image.caption: Figure 7.8.11: Finding bearing given north and east distances.

worked_examples.problem: A plane is 150 km north and 200 km east of its starting point. Find the bearing from the starting point to the plane.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with north and east components.
step_by_step.calculation:
North: 150 km (adjacent)
East: 200 km (opposite)
Angle from north: θ (unknown)

step_by_step.explanation: Have opposite and adjacent. Use tangent.

step_by_step.calculation: Bearing from start to plane: N 53.13° E

worked_examples.concept_applied: Finding bearing: draw triangle, use tan⁻¹ to find angle from north, write in bearing notation.

worked_examples.title: Two-Bearing Navigation Problem

image.caption: Figure 7.8.12: Using two bearings to find distance in navigation.

worked_examples.problem: From point A, a tower has bearing N 30° E. From point B, 10 km due east of A, the tower has bearing N 60° W. Find the distance from A to the tower.

step_by_step.calculation:
Points A and B are 10 km apart
Tower at point T
Angle at A: 30° from north
Angle at B: 60° from north

Side opposite 30° (AT) : Side opposite 60° (BT) : Hypotenuse (AB)
AB = 10 km (given)

step_by_step.explanation: AT is opposite 30°, AB is hypotenuse.

step_by_step.calculation: Distance from A to tower: 5 km

worked_examples.final_answer: Distance from A to tower: 5 km
worked_examples.concept_applied: Two-bearing problems: draw triangle, find angles, use trig or special triangles to find distances.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.13: Summary of bearings and navigation applications.

sections.introduction: Indirect measurement uses trigonometry to find measurements you can't measure directly—heights of tall buildings, widths of rivers, distances to far objects. This is one of the most practical applications of trigonometry—surveyors, engineers, and scientists use it constantly. The key is setting up a right triangle with known angles and at least one known distance.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.8.14: Introducing indirect measurement using trigonometry.

key_concept.definition:
Indirect Measurement:
Finding measurements without direct measurement
Use trigonometry with known angles and distances

Common Types:

1. Height Problems:
Find height of building, tree, mountain
Measure distance from base and angle of elevation
Use tangent ratio

2. Distance Problems:
Find distance across river, canyon, etc.
Measure baseline and angles
Use law of sines or right triangle trig

3. Shadow Problems:
Use similar triangles with shadows
Object height / shadow = known height / known shadow

4. Two-Station Problems:
Measure from two different positions
Use two angles and distance between stations
Solve system of equations

General Strategy:
1. Draw accurate diagram
2. Label all known information
3. Identify right triangle(s)
4. Choose appropriate trig ratio
5. Solve for unknown
6. Check if answer is reasonable
key_concept.context: Indirect measurement is practical—you can find heights of buildings without climbing them, widths of rivers without crossing them, distances to ships without sailing to them. This is how surveyors work, how engineers design structures, how scientists measure distant objects. The trigonometry is the same—SOH CAH TOA—but applied to real measurement problems. Understanding indirect measurement builds practical problem-solving skills—you can solve real measurement challenges. In applications, accuracy matters—small angle errors can create large distance errors. Understanding indirect measurement builds appreciation for trigonometry as practical tool. This section teaches indirect measurement—height, distance, shadow, and two-station problems.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.15: Diagram showing different types of indirect measurement problems: height, distance, shadow, and two-station problems.

worked_examples.title: Finding Height of Building

image.caption: Figure 7.8.16: Using trigonometry to find height of building indirectly.

worked_examples.problem: A surveyor stands 100 feet from a building and measures the angle of elevation to the top as 40°. If the surveyor's eye level is 5 feet above ground, find the total height of the building.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw right triangle with angle of elevation.
step_by_step.calculation:
Distance from building: 100 ft (adjacent)
Angle of elevation: 40°
Height above eye level: h (opposite)
Eye level: 5 ft

step_by_step.explanation: Add surveyor's eye level to get total height.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Height problems: use tan to find height above eye level, add eye level for total height.

worked_examples.title: Finding Width of River

image.caption: Figure 7.8.17: Using trigonometry to find width of river indirectly.

worked_examples.problem: To find the width of a river, a surveyor measures a baseline of 50 meters along one bank. From the ends of the baseline, the angles to a tree on the opposite bank are 70° and 60°. Find the width of the river.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw triangle with baseline and tree.
step_by_step.calculation:
Baseline AB = 50 m
Angle at A = 70°
Angle at B = 60°
Tree at point C

step_by_step.explanation: Use law of sines to find AC or BC.
Width is perpendicular from C to AB.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Distance problems: use law of sines to find sides, then find perpendicular distance for width.

worked_examples.title: Shadow Problem

image.caption: Figure 7.8.18: Using similar triangles with shadows to find height.

worked_examples.problem: A 6-foot person casts a 4-foot shadow. At the same time, a building casts a 30-foot shadow. Find the height of the building.

step_by_step.calculation:
Person: height 6 ft, shadow 4 ft
Building: height h, shadow 30 ft

Person height / Person shadow = Building height / Building shadow
6/4 = h/30

step_by_step.explanation: Angle of elevation is same for both.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Shadow problems: use similar triangles (proportional sides) or same angle of elevation to find height.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.19: Summary of indirect measurement applications.

sections.section_title: 7.8.4 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Applications of right triangle trigonometry introduce specific pitfalls related to diagram setup, angle identification, trig ratio selection, and unit consistency. Recognizing common mistakes helps students develop careful habits and produce accurate results. Understanding where errors typically occur enables self-correction and better problem-solving.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.8.20: Overview of common errors in trigonometry applications.

key_concept.definition:
Common Errors:

1. Diagram Errors:
- Not drawing accurate diagram
- Wrong angle placement
- Not labeling known/unknown
- Wrong right triangle identification

2. Angle Identification Errors:
- Confusing angle of elevation with depression
- Wrong angle in triangle
- Not using alternate interior angles
- Bearing angle confusion

3. Trig Ratio Errors:
- Choosing wrong trig ratio (sin vs. cos vs. tan)
- Not matching ratio to known/unknown sides
- Forgetting SOH CAH TOA

4. Unit Errors:
- Mixing units (feet and meters)
- Not converting units
- Wrong unit in final answer

5. Calculator Errors:
- Wrong calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD)
- Not using parentheses correctly
- Rounding too early

Error Prevention:
- Draw clear, labeled diagram
- Identify angle carefully (elevation vs. depression)
- Use SOH CAH TOA to choose ratio
- Keep units consistent throughout
- Check calculator mode
- Keep extra digits, round at end
- Check if answer is reasonable
key_concept.context: Application errors are common—wrong diagram leads to wrong setup, wrong angle leads to wrong trig ratio, wrong units give wrong answers. Calculator mode errors (DEG vs. RAD) give completely wrong values. Rounding too early accumulates errors. Understanding these errors develops application literacy—knowing how to set up, solve, and check. In real applications, errors mean wrong building heights, wrong distances, wrong positions. This section teaches error awareness—critical for accurate application work.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.21: Diagram showing common errors in trigonometry applications and how to avoid them.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Diagram Setup Error

image.caption: Figure 7.8.22: Correcting errors in diagram setup for applications.

worked_examples.problem: A student solved a building height problem but got 200 feet when the building is actually 50 feet. The angle was 30° and distance was 100 feet. Identify the likely error.

step_by_step.explanation: Student likely used wrong trig ratio.
step_by_step.calculation:
If student used tan 60° instead: 100 × 1.732 ≈ 173 ft
Or used cot instead of tan
Likely used wrong angle or wrong trig ratio

worked_examples.final_answer: Likely error: wrong trig ratio or wrong angle; Correct: tan 30° = h/100, h ≈ 57.7 ft
worked_examples.concept_applied: Application errors: check diagram, verify trig ratio, check calculator mode.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Angle of Elevation/Depression Error

image.caption: Figure 7.8.23: Correcting errors with angles of elevation and depression.

worked_examples.problem: A student solved an angle of depression problem but used the angle at the bottom instead of the top. Explain the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Angle of depression is measured at top (looking down)
Student used angle at bottom

step_by_step.explanation: Show where angle of depression is measured.
step_by_step.calculation:
Angle of depression at top of cliff/building
Measured from horizontal downward

step_by_step.explanation: Angle of depression = angle of elevation (alternate interior angles).
step_by_step.calculation: Angle at bottom (elevation) = angle at top (depression)
Angle of depression is at top; equals angle of elevation at bottom

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used wrong angle location; Correct: angle of depression at top = angle of elevation at bottom
worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle of elevation/depression: measure from horizontal, use alternate interior angle relationship.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Unit Consistency Error

image.caption: Figure 7.8.24: Correcting errors with unit consistency.

worked_examples.problem: A student calculated a distance as 1000 when the distance should be in meters but they mixed feet and meters. Explain the error.

step_by_step.calculation: Student mixed feet and meters in calculation

step_by_step.explanation: Convert all to same units before calculating.
step_by_step.calculation: Convert all measurements to same unit (all meters or all feet)
Convert before calculating, or convert answer at end

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: mixed units; Correct: convert all to same units before calculating
worked_examples.concept_applied: Unit consistency: keep all measurements in same units throughout calculation.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.25: Summary of common errors in trigonometry applications.

sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to trigonometry applications—angles of elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement, and error avoidance—to demonstrate mastery. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for real-world problem-solving.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 7.8.26: Practice problems covering trigonometry applications.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) Angle of elevation is measured from horizontal looking up or down?
2) Angle of depression from top equals what angle at bottom?
3) Bearing N 45° E means 45° east of what?
4) Building 100 ft away, elevation 45°, find height.
5) Cliff 50 m high, depression 30°, find distance.
6) Shadow 10 ft, sun angle 60°, find object height.
7) Ship sails N 30° E 100 km, find north component.
8) Same ship, find east component.
9) Two angles 40° and 50°, baseline 100 m, find distance.
10) Person 6 ft, shadow 4 ft; tree shadow 20 ft, find tree height.
11) Angle of elevation uses which trig ratio for height?
12) Bearing S 60° W means 60° west of what?
13) Ladder 20 ft, angle 70°, find height on wall.
14) Same ladder, find distance from wall.
15) From 50 m, elevation 30°, find building height.
16) Plane 1000 m high, depression 45°, find ground distance.
17) Bearing N 0° E is what direction?
18) Bearing S 0° W is what direction?
19) Why draw diagram for applications?
20) What to check after solving application?

step_by_step.step: 1) Angle of elevation measured from horizontal
7) Ship N 30° E 100 km, north component

step_by_step.explanation: Use law of sines or right triangle trig.
step_by_step.calculation: Depends on setup; typically 50-100 m range

step_by_step.step: 10) Person 6 ft, shadow 4 ft; tree shadow 20 ft
11) Angle of elevation uses which ratio for height
13) Ladder 20 ft, angle 70°, height on wall

step_by_step.calculation: Visualize problem, identify right triangle, label known/unknown
Check if answer is reasonable, check units, check calculation

worked_examples.final_answer: 1) Up; 2) Angle of elevation; 3) North; 4) 100 ft; 5) 86.6 m; 6) 17.32 ft; 7) 86.6 km; 8) 50 km; 9) 50-100 m; 10) 30 ft; 11) Tangent; 12) South; 13) 18.79 ft; 14) 6.84 ft; 15) 28.87 m; 16) 1000 m; 17) Due north; 18) Due south; 19) Visualize problem; 20) Check reasonableness
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of trigonometry applications—elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement.

image.caption: Figure 7.8.27: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Applications of right triangle trig: angles of elevation (looking up from horizontal), depression (looking down from horizontal), bearings (N θ E, S θ W, etc.), indirect measurement (heights, distances, shadows). Elevation/depression are equal (alternate interior angles). Bearings measured from north or south toward east or west. Problem-solving strategy: draw diagram, label known/unknown, identify right triangle, choose trig ratio (SOH CAH TOA), solve, check. Common errors: wrong diagram, wrong angle, wrong trig ratio, unit mixing, calculator mode. Applications in surveying, navigation, construction, physics. Essential for real-world problem-solving with trigonometry.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive summary: trigonometry applications, angles of elevation/depression, bearings, indirect measurement, problem-solving strategy, common errors
review_questions_image.caption: Figure 7.8.28: Complete reference for trigonometry applications showing elevation/depression angles, bearing notation, indirect measurement types, and problem-solving strategy.
